1→# Authentication and Authorization 2→ 3→The MCP Gateway Registry provides authentication and authorization using industry-standard OAuth 2.0 flows with fine-grained access control. 4→ 5→## Overview 6→ 7→The authentication system supports three distinct identity scenarios: 8→ 9→1. **Human Users** - Interactive users accessing the Registry UI via browser 10→2. **Programmatic Access** - Self-signed JWT tokens for CLI tools and AI coding assistants 11→3. **Workload Identity (M2M)** - Service accounts for AI agents and automated systems 12→ 13→## Related Documentation 14→ 15→### Design Documents 16→ 17→For architectural details and design decisions: 18→ 19→- [Authentication Design](design/authentication-design.md) - Detailed auth flows for human users, programmatic access, and M2M workloads 20→- [Multi-Provider IdP Support](design/idp-provider-support.md) - Architecture for supporting multiple identity providers (Keycloak, Entra ID) 21→ 22→### Configuration Guides 23→ 24→For setup and configuration: 25→ 26→- [Scopes Management](scopes-mgmt.md) - Scope configuration file format and fine-grained access control 27→- [Authentication Management](auth-mgmt.md) - Managing users, groups, and scopes via CLI 28→- [Microsoft Entra ID Setup](entra-id-setup.md) - Entra ID-specific setup and configuration 29→- [Complete Setup Guide](complete-setup-guide.md) - End-to-end deployment instructions 30→ 31→--- 32→ 33→## Authentication Architecture 34→ 35→### Identity Types 36→ 37→The system distinguishes between three types of identities, each with different authentication flows: 38→ 39→| Identity Type | Use Case | Auth Method | Token Signing | Lifetime | 40→|--------------|----------|-------------|---------------|----------| 41→| Human Users | Browser UI | OAuth2 Authorization Code | RS256 (IdP) | Session-based | 42→| Programmatic | CLI, AI assistants | Self-signed JWT | HS256 (SECRET_KEY) | 8 hours | 43→| M2M Workloads | AI agents, automation | OAuth2 Client Credentials | RS256 (IdP) | 1 hour | 44→ 45→### Supported Identity Providers 46→ 47→The registry supports multiple identity providers through a pluggable architecture: 48→ 49→- **Keycloak** - Open-source identity management 50→- **Microsoft Entra ID** - Enterprise Azure AD integration 51→ 52→Provider selection is controlled by the `AUTH_PROVIDER` environment variable: 53→ 54→```bash 55→AUTH_PROVIDER=keycloak # Use Keycloak (default) 56→AUTH_PROVIDER=entra # Use Microsoft Entra ID 57→``` 58→ 59→--- 60→ 61→## High-Level Authentication Flow 62→ 63→```mermaid 64→sequenceDiagram 65→ participant User as User/Developer 66→ participant Agent as AI Agent 67→ participant Auth as Keycloak/Entra ID
(Identity Provider) 68→ participant Gateway as NGINX Gateway 69→ participant AuthServer as Auth Server 70→ participant Registry as Registry API 71→ 72→ Note over User,Registry: Human User Flow (Browser) 73→ 74→ User->>Gateway: 1. Access Registry UI 75→ Gateway->>Auth: 2. Redirect to IdP login 76→ Auth->>User: 3. Login page 77→ User->>Auth: 4. Authenticate 78→ Auth->>Gateway: 5. Authorization code 79→ Gateway->>AuthServer: 6. Exchange code for tokens 80→ AuthServer->>Auth: 7. Validate & get user info 81→ AuthServer->>User: 8. Set session cookie 82→ User->>Registry: 9. Access API with session 83→ 84→ Note over User,Registry: Programmatic Access (JWT Token) 85→ 86→ User->>AuthServer: 10. Request JWT token (via UI) 87→ AuthServer->>User: 11. Self-signed JWT (HS256) 88→ User->>Agent: 12. Configure agent with token 89→ Agent->>Gateway: 13. API request with Bearer token 90→ Gateway->>AuthServer: 14. Validate token (/validate) 91→ AuthServer->>Gateway: 15. User context + permissions 92→ Gateway->>Registry: 16. Proxied request 93→``` 94→ 95→--- 96→ 97→## Authorization Model 98→ 99→### Scope-Based Access Control 100→ 101→Authorization is based on **scopes** that define: 102→ 103→1. **Server Access** - Which MCP servers and methods users can access 104→2. **Agent Actions** - Which agent operations users can perform 105→3. **UI Permissions** - Which UI features are available 106→ 107→### Group-to-Scope Mapping 108→ 109→User permissions are determined by mapping IdP groups to scopes stored in MongoDB/DocumentDB: 110→ 111→```mermaid 112→flowchart LR 113→ subgraph IdP["Identity Provider"] 114→ KC["Keycloak Group
registry-admins"] 115→ EA["Entra ID Group
4c46ec66-a4f7-..."] 116→ end 117→ 118→ subgraph DB["MongoDB/DocumentDB"] 119→ SM["Scope: registry-admins
group_mappings:
- registry-admins
- 4c46ec66-a4f7-..."] 120→ end 121→ 122→ subgraph Perms["Permissions"] 123→ SA["server_access:
server: *
methods: [all]
tools: [all]"] 124→ UI["ui_permissions:
list_agents: [all]
publish_agent: [all]
..."] 125→ end 126→ 127→ KC --> SM 128→ EA --> SM 129→ SM --> SA 130→ SM --> UI 131→``` 132→ 133→### Scope Configuration 134→ 135→Scopes are defined in JSON files and loaded into MongoDB. See [Scopes Management](scopes-mgmt.md) for the complete file format. 136→ 137→**Example: Admin Scope** 138→ 139→```json 140→{ 141→ "_id": "registry-admins", 142→ "group_mappings": ["registry-admins", "4c46ec66-a4f7-4b62-9095-b7958662f4b6"], 143→ "server_access": [ 144→ {"server": "*", "methods": ["all"], "tools": ["all"]} 145→ ], 146→ "ui_permissions": { 147→ "list_agents": ["all"], 148→ "publish_agent": ["all"], 149→ "list_service": ["all"], 150→ "toggle_service": ["all"] 151→ } 152→} 153→``` 154→ 155→**Example: Limited User Scope** 156→ 157→```json 158→{ 159→ "_id": "public-mcp-users", 160→ "group_mappings": ["public-mcp-users", "5f605d68-06bc-4208-b992-bb378eee12c5"], 161→ "server_access": [ 162→ {"server": "context7", "methods": ["initialize", "tools/list", "tools/call"], "tools": ["*"]} 163→ ], 164→ "ui_permissions": { 165→ "list_service": ["all"], 166→ "list_agents": ["/flight-booking"], 167→ "get_agent": ["/flight-booking"] 168→ } 169→} 170→``` 171→ 172→--- 173→ 174→## Token Validation Flow 175→ 176→All API requests are validated by the auth server through NGINX's `auth_request` directive: 177→ 178→```mermaid 179→sequenceDiagram 180→ participant Client as CLI/Agent 181→ participant NGINX as NGINX Gateway 182→ participant Auth as Auth Server 183→ participant IdP as Identity Provider 184→ participant API as Registry API 185→ 186→ Client->>NGINX: 1. API Request
Authorization: Bearer 187→ NGINX->>Auth: 2. auth_request /validate 188→ 189→ alt Self-Signed Token (iss: mcp-auth-server) 190→ Auth->>Auth: 3a. Validate with SECRET_KEY (HS256) 191→ else IdP Token (iss: Keycloak/Entra) 192→ Auth->>IdP: 3b. Fetch JWKS 193→ IdP-->>Auth: Public keys 194→ Auth->>Auth: 3c. Validate signature (RS256) 195→ end 196→ 197→ Auth->>Auth: 4. Extract groups from token 198→ Auth->>Auth: 5. Map groups to scopes 199→ Auth->>Auth: 6. Check server/tool access 200→ 201→ alt Access Granted 202→ Auth-->>NGINX: 7a. 200 OK + X-User headers 203→ NGINX->>API: 8. Proxy request 204→ API-->>Client: 9. Response 205→ else Access Denied 206→ Auth-->>NGINX: 7b. 403 Forbidden 207→ NGINX-->>Client: 403 Forbidden 208→ end 209→``` 210→ 211→--- 212→ 213→## Key Security Layers 214→ 215→### Layer 1: Gateway Authentication 216→ 217→The NGINX gateway validates all incoming requests: 218→ 219→- Extracts JWT from `Authorization` header 220→- Calls auth server `/validate` endpoint 221→- Sets user context headers for downstream services 222→ 223→### Layer 2: Token Validation 224→ 225→The auth server supports multiple token types: 226→ 227→| Token Type | Issuer | Algorithm | Validation Method | 228→|------------|--------|-----------|-------------------| 229→| Self-signed | `mcp-auth-server` | HS256 | SECRET_KEY | 230→| Keycloak | `{keycloak_url}/realms/{realm}` | RS256 | JWKS endpoint | 231→| Entra ID | `https://sts.windows.net/{tenant}/` | RS256 | JWKS endpoint | 232→ 233→### Layer 3: Scope-Based Authorization 234→ 235→After token validation, the auth server: 236→ 237→1. Extracts `groups` claim from token 238→2. Queries MongoDB for matching scopes (via `group_mappings`) 239→3. Validates requested server/method/tool against `server_access` rules 240→4. Returns user context with permissions 241→ 242→--- 243→ 244→## Permission Types 245→ 246→### MCP Server Permissions 247→ 248→Control access to MCP servers and their tools: 249→ 250→| Permission | Description | 251→|------------|-------------| 252→| `server` | Server name or `*` for all | 253→| `methods` | Allowed MCP methods (initialize, tools/list, tools/call, etc.) | 254→| `tools` | Allowed tool names or `*` for all | 255→ 256→### Agent Permissions 257→ 258→Control operations on A2A agents: 259→ 260→| Permission | Description | 261→|------------|-------------| 262→| `list_agents` | View agents in listings | 263→| `get_agent` | View agent details | 264→| `publish_agent` | Register new agents | 265→| `modify_agent` | Update existing agents | 266→| `delete_agent` | Remove agents | 267→ 268→### UI Permissions 269→ 270→Control access to UI features: 271→ 272→| Permission | Description | 273→|------------|-------------| 274→| `list_service` | View MCP servers in dashboard | 275→| `register_service` | Register new MCP servers | 276→| `health_check_service` | Run health checks | 277→| `toggle_service` | Enable/disable servers | 278→| `modify_service` | Edit server configurations | 279→ 280→--- 281→ 282→## Entra ID Group Mapping 283→ 284→When using Microsoft Entra ID, group identifiers are Object IDs (GUIDs), not names: 285→ 286→```json 287→{ 288→ "group_mappings": [ 289→ "public-mcp-users", 290→ "5f605d68-06bc-4208-b992-bb378eee12c5" 291→ ] 292→} 293→``` 294→ 295→This allows the same scope to work with both Keycloak (group names) and Entra ID (Object IDs). 296→ 297→**Finding Entra ID Group Object IDs:** 298→ 299→1. Azure Portal > Azure Active Directory > Groups 300→2. Select the group 301→3. Copy the "Object ID" from the Overview page 302→ 303→--- 304→ 305→## Session Management 306→ 307→### Human User Sessions 308→ 309→Browser sessions use signed cookies: 310→ 311→- Created after successful OAuth2 login 312→- Contains: username, groups, provider, scopes 313→- Validated using `SECRET_KEY` (HS256) 314→- Default expiry: 8 hours (configurable) 315→ 316→### Programmatic Tokens 317→ 318→Self-signed JWT tokens for CLI/API access: 319→ 320→- Generated via "Get JWT Token" in UI 321→- Contains: username, groups, scopes, permissions 322→- Signed with `SECRET_KEY` (HS256) 323→- Default expiry: 8 hours 324→ 325→### M2M Tokens 326→ 327→Service account tokens from IdP: 328→ 329→- Obtained via OAuth2 Client Credentials flow 330→- Signed by IdP (RS256) 331→- Default expiry: 1 hour 332→- Must be refreshed periodically 333→ 334→--- 335→ 336→## Security Best Practices 337→ 338→1. **Use HTTPS** - All production deployments should use TLS 339→2. **Rotate Secrets** - Regularly rotate SECRET_KEY and client secrets 340→3. **Least Privilege** - Assign minimal required permissions to users/agents 341→4. **Audit Logging** - Monitor authentication events and access patterns 342→5. **Token Expiry** - Use short-lived tokens and implement refresh flows 343→ 344→--- 345→ 346→## Troubleshooting 347→ 348→### Common Issues 349→ 350→**Token validation fails:** 351→- Check token issuer matches expected provider 352→- Verify JWKS endpoint is accessible 353→- Ensure SECRET_KEY matches between auth server instances 354→ 355→**Permission denied:** 356→- Verify user's groups in IdP 357→- Check group_mappings in scope configuration 358→- Ensure scope includes required server/method access 359→ 360→**Group not recognized:** 361→- For Entra ID: Use Object ID, not group name 362→- Verify group exists in group_mappings array 363→- Reload scopes after configuration changes 364→ 365→### Debug Endpoints 366→ 367→```bash 368→# Check user context 369→curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 370→ https://registry.example.com/api/debug/user-context 371→ 372→# List available scopes 373→curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 374→ https://registry.example.com/api/scopes 375→``` 376→ 377→--- 378→ 379→## Server Authentication Credentials 380→ 381→The MCP Gateway Registry supports backend server authentication, allowing MCP servers that require their own authentication (API keys, bearer tokens, etc.) to be registered with encrypted credentials. 382→ 383→### Overview 384→ 385→When an MCP server requires authentication, you can provide the credentials during registration. The registry: 386→ 387→1. **Encrypts** the credential using Fernet symmetric encryption 388→2. **Stores** the encrypted credential in MongoDB/DocumentDB 389→3. **Automatically decrypts** and uses the credential for: 390→ - Health checks 391→ - Tool discovery and fetching 392→ - MCP client connections 393→ 394→### Supported Authentication Schemes 395→ 396→| Scheme | Description | Example Use Case | 397→|--------|-------------|------------------| 398→| `none` | No authentication required | Public MCP servers | 399→| `bearer` | Bearer token in `Authorization` header | OAuth2-protected services | 400→| `api_key` | API key with custom header name | Services requiring API keys (e.g., `X-API-Key`, `CONTEXT7_API_KEY`) | 401→ 402→### Credential Encryption 403→ 404→All credentials are encrypted before storage using the Fernet encryption scheme: 405→ 406→- **Algorithm**: Fernet (symmetric encryption based on AES-128-CBC) 407→- **Key**: Derived from `ENCRYPTION_KEY` environment variable 408→- **Storage**: Encrypted credential stored as `auth_credential_encrypted` in MongoDB 409→- **Decryption**: Automatic during health checks and MCP client initialization 410→ 411→**Configuration** (`.env` file): 412→```bash 413→# Encryption key for server credentials (base64-encoded Fernet key) 414→ENCRYPTION_KEY=your-base64-encoded-fernet-key-here 415→ 416→# Generate a new key with: python -c "from cryptography.fernet import Fernet; print(Fernet.generate_key().decode())" 417→``` 418→ 419→### Registering Servers with Authentication 420→ 421→#### Method 1: Registry UI 422→ 423→1. Navigate to **Register Server** in the Registry UI 424→2. Fill in server details (name, path, proxy URL, etc.) 425→3. Select **Authentication Scheme**: 426→ - `none` - No authentication 427→ - `bearer` - Bearer token 428→ - `api_key` - API key 429→4. If `bearer` or `api_key`: 430→ - Enter the **credential** (API key or bearer token) 431→ - For `api_key`: Specify the **header name** (e.g., `CONTEXT7_API_KEY`, `X-API-Key`) 432→5. Click **Register** 433→ 434→![Server Registration with Authentication Scheme](img/auth-scheme.gif) 435→ 436→The credential is automatically encrypted and stored securely. 437→ 438→#### Method 2: REST API 439→ 440→**Register server with bearer token:** 441→ 442→```bash 443→curl -X POST https://registry.example.com/api/servers/register \ 444→ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 445→ -F "server_name=My Protected Server" \ 446→ -F "path=/my-server" \ 447→ -F "proxy_pass_url=http://backend:8000/" \ 448→ -F "auth_scheme=bearer" \ 449→ -F "auth_credential=my-bearer-token-value" \ 450→ -F "description=A server requiring bearer auth" 451→``` 452→ 453→**Register server with API key:** 454→ 455→```bash 456→curl -X POST https://registry.example.com/api/servers/register \ 457→ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 458→ -F "server_name=Context7" \ 459→ -F "path=/context7" \ 460→ -F "proxy_pass_url=http://context7:8000/" \ 461→ -F "auth_scheme=api_key" \ 462→ -F "auth_credential=ctx7sk-6dd75bd4-80ef-486e-99ef-b5493df4e578" \ 463→ -F "auth_header_name=CONTEXT7_API_KEY" \ 464→ -F "description=Context7 LLM context service" 465→``` 466→ 467→**Response:** 468→```json 469→{ 470→ "message": "Server registered successfully", 471→ "path": "/context7", 472→ "server_name": "Context7", 473→ "auth_scheme": "api_key", 474→ "auth_header_name": "CONTEXT7_API_KEY", 475→ "auth_credential_encrypted": true 476→} 477→``` 478→ 479→#### Method 3: CLI Tool (`registry_management.py`) 480→ 481→**Register server with credentials:** 482→ 483→```bash 484→# Set up authentication 485→export REGISTRY_URL=https://registry.example.com 486→export REGISTRY_TOKEN=$(cat .token) 487→ 488→# Register with API key 489→python3 api/registry_management.py \ 490→ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ 491→ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ 492→ server-register \ 493→ --name "Context7" \ 494→ --path "/context7" \ 495→ --proxy-pass-url "http://context7:8000/" \ 496→ --auth-scheme api_key \ 497→ --auth-credential "ctx7sk-6dd75bd4-80ef-486e-99ef-b5493df4e578" \ 498→ --auth-header-name "CONTEXT7_API_KEY" \ 499→ --description "Context7 LLM context service" 500→ 501→# Register with bearer token 502→python3 api/registry_management.py \ 503→ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ 504→ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ 505→ server-register \ 506→ --name "Cloudflare API" \ 507→ --path "/cloudflare-api" \ 508→ --proxy-pass-url "http://cloudflare-mcp:8000/" \ 509→ --auth-scheme bearer \ 510→ --auth-credential "my-cloudflare-bearer-token" \ 511→ --description "Cloudflare MCP Server" 512→``` 513→ 514→### Updating Server Credentials 515→ 516→Credentials can be updated without re-registering the entire server. 517→ 518→#### Method 1: REST API 519→ 520→**Update credential endpoint:** 521→ 522→```bash 523→curl -X PUT https://registry.example.com/api/servers/context7/credentials \ 524→ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 525→ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ 526→ -d '{ 527→ "auth_scheme": "api_key", 528→ "auth_credential": "new-api-key-value", 529→ "auth_header_name": "CONTEXT7_API_KEY" 530→ }' 531→``` 532→ 533→**Switch to bearer token:** 534→ 535→```bash 536→curl -X PUT https://registry.example.com/api/servers/my-server/credentials \ 537→ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 538→ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ 539→ -d '{ 540→ "auth_scheme": "bearer", 541→ "auth_credential": "new-bearer-token" 542→ }' 543→``` 544→ 545→**Remove authentication:** 546→ 547→```bash 548→curl -X PUT https://registry.example.com/api/servers/my-server/credentials \ 549→ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 550→ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ 551→ -d '{ 552→ "auth_scheme": "none" 553→ }' 554→``` 555→ 556→**Response:** 557→```json 558→{ 559→ "message": "Auth credentials updated successfully", 560→ "path": "/context7", 561→ "auth_scheme": "api_key", 562→ "auth_header_name": "CONTEXT7_API_KEY" 563→} 564→``` 565→ 566→#### Method 2: CLI Tool 567→ 568→**Update server credential:** 569→ 570→```bash 571→python3 api/registry_management.py \ 572→ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ 573→ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ 574→ server-update-credential \ 575→ --path "/context7" \ 576→ --auth-scheme api_key \ 577→ --credential "new-api-key-value" \ 578→ --auth-header-name "CONTEXT7_API_KEY" 579→``` 580→ 581→**Update to bearer token:** 582→ 583→```bash 584→python3 api/registry_management.py \ 585→ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ 586→ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ 587→ server-update-credential \ 588→ --path "/cloudflare-api" \ 589→ --auth-scheme bearer \ 590→ --credential "new-bearer-token-value" 591→``` 592→ 593→**Remove authentication:** 594→ 595→```bash 596→python3 api/registry_management.py \ 597→ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ 598→ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ 599→ server-update-credential \ 600→ --path "/my-server" \ 601→ --auth-scheme none 602→``` 603→ 604→**Output:** 605→``` 606→Successfully updated credentials for server '/context7' 607→New auth scheme: api_key 608→Auth header name: CONTEXT7_API_KEY 609→``` 610→ 611→### How Credentials Are Used 612→ 613→#### 1. Health Checks 614→ 615→When the health check service performs periodic checks, it: 616→ 617→1. Retrieves the server's `auth_credential_encrypted` from MongoDB 618→2. Decrypts the credential using the `ENCRYPTION_KEY` 619→3. Includes the appropriate header in the MCP initialize request: 620→ - Bearer: `Authorization: Bearer ` 621→ - API Key: `: ` 622→ 623→**Example health check with auth:** 624→ 625→```python 626→# Health check service automatically decrypts and uses credentials 627→headers = {} 628→if server.auth_scheme == "bearer": 629→ headers["Authorization"] = f"Bearer {decrypt_credential(server.auth_credential_encrypted)}" 630→elif server.auth_scheme == "api_key": 631→ header_name = server.auth_header_name or "X-API-Key" 632→ headers[header_name] = decrypt_credential(server.auth_credential_encrypted) 633→ 634→# MCP initialize request with auth headers 635→response = await mcp_client.initialize(url=server.proxy_pass_url, headers=headers) 636→``` 637→ 638→#### 2. Tool Discovery 639→ 640→When fetching tools from a server: 641→ 642→1. Registry decrypts the credential 643→2. Includes auth headers in the MCP `tools/list` request 644→3. Stores the fetched tools in the database 645→ 646→#### 3. MCP Client Connections 647→ 648→When AI coding assistants connect to a server through the gateway: 649→ 650→1. User provides gateway auth token (`X-Authorization` header) 651→2. Gateway validates user permissions 652→3. Gateway retrieves and decrypts server credential 653→4. Gateway proxies the request with the server's auth header 654→ 655→**Example MCP client configuration:** 656→ 657→```json 658→{ 659→ "mcpServers": { 660→ "context7": { 661→ "type": "streamable-http", 662→ "url": "https://mcpgateway.ddns.net/context7/mcp", 663→ "disabled": false, 664→ "headers": { 665→ "X-Authorization": "Bearer ", 666→ "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "" 667→ } 668→ } 669→ } 670→} 671→``` 672→ 673→### Security Considerations 674→ 675→1. **Encryption at Rest**: 676→ - All credentials are encrypted in MongoDB using Fernet 677→ - Never store plaintext credentials in the database 678→ 679→2. **Key Management**: 680→ - Store `ENCRYPTION_KEY` securely (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, etc.) 681→ - Never commit encryption keys to version control 682→ - Rotate encryption keys periodically 683→ 684→3. **Access Control**: 685→ - Only users with `register_service` or `modify_service` permissions can set/update credentials 686→ - Credentials are never returned in API responses (only `auth_credential_encrypted` flag) 687→ 688→4. **Audit Logging**: 689→ - All credential updates are logged with username and timestamp 690→ - Review audit logs regularly for unauthorized changes 691→ 692→### Best Practices 693→ 694→1. **Use Environment-Specific Credentials**: 695→ - Development: Use test credentials with limited access 696→ - Production: Use production credentials with full access 697→ 698→2. **Rotate Credentials Regularly**: 699→ - Use the credential update API/CLI to rotate without downtime 700→ - Update credentials before they expire 701→ 702→3. **Monitor Health Checks**: 703→ - Watch for "auth-expired" health status 704→ - Set up alerts for authentication failures 705→ 706→4. **Document Custom Headers**: 707→ - For `api_key` auth, document the required header name 708→ - Ensure consistency across environments 709→ 710→### Troubleshooting 711→ 712→**Credential Update Fails:** 713→```bash 714→# Verify server exists 715→curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 716→ https://registry.example.com/api/servers 717→ 718→# Check auth scheme is valid 719→# Valid values: none, bearer, api_key 720→``` 721→ 722→**Health Check Shows "auth-expired":** 723→```bash 724→# Update the credential 725→python3 api/registry_management.py \ 726→ server-update-credential \ 727→ --path "/my-server" \ 728→ --auth-scheme bearer \ 729→ --credential "new-valid-token" 730→ 731→# Force immediate health check 732→curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ 733→ https://registry.example.com/api/servers/my-server/refresh 734→``` 735→ 736→**MCP Client Connection Fails:** 737→- Verify both gateway auth (`X-Authorization`) and server auth headers are present 738→- Check credential hasn't expired 739→- Ensure `auth_header_name` matches server's expectation 740→ 741→--- 742→ 743→## Additional Resources 744→ 745→- [Authentication Design](design/authentication-design.md) - Detailed auth flow diagrams 746→- [IdP Provider Support](design/idp-provider-support.md) - Provider architecture 747→- [Scopes Management](scopes-mgmt.md) - Scope file format reference 748→- [Auth Management](auth-mgmt.md) - CLI operations guide 749→- [AI Coding Assistants Setup](ai-coding-assistants-setup.md) - Complete setup with backend auth examples 750→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

# Authentication and Authorization The MCP Gateway Registry provides authentication and authorization using industry-standard OAuth 2.0 flows with fine-grained access control. ## Overview The authentication system supports three distinct identity scenarios: 1. **Human Users** - Interactive users accessing the Registry UI via browser 2. **Programmatic Access** - Self-signed JWT tokens for CLI tools and AI coding assistants 3. **Workload Identity (M2M)** - Service accounts for AI agents and automated systems ## Related Documentation ### Design Documents For architectural details and design decisions: - [Authentication Design](design/authentication-design.md) - Detailed auth flows for human users, programmatic access, and M2M workloads - [Multi-Provider IdP Support](design/idp-provider-support.md) - Architecture for supporting multiple identity providers (Keycloak, Entra ID) ### Configuration Guides For setup and configuration: - [Scopes Management](scopes-mgmt.md) - Scope configuration file format and fine-grained access control - [Authentication Management](auth-mgmt.md) - Managing users, groups, and scopes via CLI - [Microsoft Entra ID Setup](entra-id-setup.md) - Entra ID-specific setup and configuration - [Complete Setup Guide](complete-setup-guide.md) - End-to-end deployment instructions --- ## Authentication Architecture ### Identity Types The system distinguishes between three types of identities, each with different authentication flows: | Identity Type | Use Case | Auth Method | Token Signing | Lifetime | |--------------|----------|-------------|---------------|----------| | Human Users | Browser UI | OAuth2 Authorization Code | RS256 (IdP) | Session-based | | Programmatic | CLI, AI assistants | Self-signed JWT | HS256 (SECRET_KEY) | 8 hours | | M2M Workloads | AI agents, automation | OAuth2 Client Credentials | RS256 (IdP) | 1 hour | ### Supported Identity Providers The registry supports multiple identity providers through a pluggable architecture: - **Keycloak** - Open-source identity management - **Microsoft Entra ID** - Enterprise Azure AD integration Provider selection is controlled by the `AUTH_PROVIDER` environment variable: ```bash AUTH_PROVIDER=keycloak # Use Keycloak (default) AUTH_PROVIDER=entra # Use Microsoft Entra ID ``` --- ## High-Level Authentication Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User as User/Developer participant Agent as AI Agent participant Auth as Keycloak/Entra ID
(Identity Provider) participant Gateway as NGINX Gateway participant AuthServer as Auth Server participant Registry as Registry API Note over User,Registry: Human User Flow (Browser) User->>Gateway: 1. Access Registry UI Gateway->>Auth: 2. Redirect to IdP login Auth->>User: 3. Login page User->>Auth: 4. Authenticate Auth->>Gateway: 5. Authorization code Gateway->>AuthServer: 6. Exchange code for tokens AuthServer->>Auth: 7. Validate & get user info AuthServer->>User: 8. Set session cookie User->>Registry: 9. Access API with session Note over User,Registry: Programmatic Access (JWT Token) User->>AuthServer: 10. Request JWT token (via UI) AuthServer->>User: 11. Self-signed JWT (HS256) User->>Agent: 12. Configure agent with token Agent->>Gateway: 13. API request with Bearer token Gateway->>AuthServer: 14. Validate token (/validate) AuthServer->>Gateway: 15. User context + permissions Gateway->>Registry: 16. Proxied request ``` --- ## Authorization Model ### Scope-Based Access Control Authorization is based on **scopes** that define: 1. **Server Access** - Which MCP servers and methods users can access 2. **Agent Actions** - Which agent operations users can perform 3. **UI Permissions** - Which UI features are available ### Group-to-Scope Mapping User permissions are determined by mapping IdP groups to scopes stored in MongoDB/DocumentDB: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IdP["Identity Provider"] KC["Keycloak Group
registry-admins"] EA["Entra ID Group
4c46ec66-a4f7-..."] end subgraph DB["MongoDB/DocumentDB"] SM["Scope: registry-admins
group_mappings:
- registry-admins
- 4c46ec66-a4f7-..."] end subgraph Perms["Permissions"] SA["server_access:
server: *
methods: [all]
tools: [all]"] UI["ui_permissions:
list_agents: [all]
publish_agent: [all]
..."] end KC --> SM EA --> SM SM --> SA SM --> UI ``` ### Scope Configuration Scopes are defined in JSON files and loaded into MongoDB. See [Scopes Management](scopes-mgmt.md) for the complete file format. **Example: Admin Scope** ```json { "_id": "registry-admins", "group_mappings": ["registry-admins", "4c46ec66-a4f7-4b62-9095-b7958662f4b6"], "server_access": [ {"server": "*", "methods": ["all"], "tools": ["all"]} ], "ui_permissions": { "list_agents": ["all"], "publish_agent": ["all"], "list_service": ["all"], "toggle_service": ["all"] } } ``` **Example: Limited User Scope** ```json { "_id": "public-mcp-users", "group_mappings": ["public-mcp-users", "5f605d68-06bc-4208-b992-bb378eee12c5"], "server_access": [ {"server": "context7", "methods": ["initialize", "tools/list", "tools/call"], "tools": ["*"]} ], "ui_permissions": { "list_service": ["all"], "list_agents": ["/flight-booking"], "get_agent": ["/flight-booking"] } } ``` --- ## Token Validation Flow All API requests are validated by the auth server through NGINX's `auth_request` directive: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as CLI/Agent participant NGINX as NGINX Gateway participant Auth as Auth Server participant IdP as Identity Provider participant API as Registry API Client->>NGINX: 1. API Request
Authorization: Bearer NGINX->>Auth: 2. auth_request /validate alt Self-Signed Token (iss: mcp-auth-server) Auth->>Auth: 3a. Validate with SECRET_KEY (HS256) else IdP Token (iss: Keycloak/Entra) Auth->>IdP: 3b. Fetch JWKS IdP-->>Auth: Public keys Auth->>Auth: 3c. Validate signature (RS256) end Auth->>Auth: 4. Extract groups from token Auth->>Auth: 5. Map groups to scopes Auth->>Auth: 6. Check server/tool access alt Access Granted Auth-->>NGINX: 7a. 200 OK + X-User headers NGINX->>API: 8. Proxy request API-->>Client: 9. Response else Access Denied Auth-->>NGINX: 7b. 403 Forbidden NGINX-->>Client: 403 Forbidden end ``` --- ## Key Security Layers ### Layer 1: Gateway Authentication The NGINX gateway validates all incoming requests: - Extracts JWT from `Authorization` header - Calls auth server `/validate` endpoint - Sets user context headers for downstream services ### Layer 2: Token Validation The auth server supports multiple token types: | Token Type | Issuer | Algorithm | Validation Method | |------------|--------|-----------|-------------------| | Self-signed | `mcp-auth-server` | HS256 | SECRET_KEY | | Keycloak | `{keycloak_url}/realms/{realm}` | RS256 | JWKS endpoint | | Entra ID | `https://sts.windows.net/{tenant}/` | RS256 | JWKS endpoint | ### Layer 3: Scope-Based Authorization After token validation, the auth server: 1. Extracts `groups` claim from token 2. Queries MongoDB for matching scopes (via `group_mappings`) 3. Validates requested server/method/tool against `server_access` rules 4. Returns user context with permissions --- ## Permission Types ### MCP Server Permissions Control access to MCP servers and their tools: | Permission | Description | |------------|-------------| | `server` | Server name or `*` for all | | `methods` | Allowed MCP methods (initialize, tools/list, tools/call, etc.) | | `tools` | Allowed tool names or `*` for all | ### Agent Permissions Control operations on A2A agents: | Permission | Description | |------------|-------------| | `list_agents` | View agents in listings | | `get_agent` | View agent details | | `publish_agent` | Register new agents | | `modify_agent` | Update existing agents | | `delete_agent` | Remove agents | ### UI Permissions Control access to UI features: | Permission | Description | |------------|-------------| | `list_service` | View MCP servers in dashboard | | `register_service` | Register new MCP servers | | `health_check_service` | Run health checks | | `toggle_service` | Enable/disable servers | | `modify_service` | Edit server configurations | --- ## Entra ID Group Mapping When using Microsoft Entra ID, group identifiers are Object IDs (GUIDs), not names: ```json { "group_mappings": [ "public-mcp-users", "5f605d68-06bc-4208-b992-bb378eee12c5" ] } ``` This allows the same scope to work with both Keycloak (group names) and Entra ID (Object IDs). **Finding Entra ID Group Object IDs:** 1. Azure Portal > Azure Active Directory > Groups 2. Select the group 3. Copy the "Object ID" from the Overview page --- ## Session Management ### Human User Sessions Browser sessions use signed cookies: - Created after successful OAuth2 login - Contains: username, groups, provider, scopes - Validated using `SECRET_KEY` (HS256) - Default expiry: 8 hours (configurable) ### Programmatic Tokens Self-signed JWT tokens for CLI/API access: - Generated via "Get JWT Token" in UI - Contains: username, groups, scopes, permissions - Signed with `SECRET_KEY` (HS256) - Default expiry: 8 hours ### M2M Tokens Service account tokens from IdP: - Obtained via OAuth2 Client Credentials flow - Signed by IdP (RS256) - Default expiry: 1 hour - Must be refreshed periodically --- ## Security Best Practices 1. **Use HTTPS** - All production deployments should use TLS 2. **Rotate Secrets** - Regularly rotate SECRET_KEY and client secrets 3. **Least Privilege** - Assign minimal required permissions to users/agents 4. **Audit Logging** - Monitor authentication events and access patterns 5. **Token Expiry** - Use short-lived tokens and implement refresh flows --- ## Troubleshooting ### Common Issues **Token validation fails:** - Check token issuer matches expected provider - Verify JWKS endpoint is accessible - Ensure SECRET_KEY matches between auth server instances **Permission denied:** - Verify user's groups in IdP - Check group_mappings in scope configuration - Ensure scope includes required server/method access **Group not recognized:** - For Entra ID: Use Object ID, not group name - Verify group exists in group_mappings array - Reload scopes after configuration changes ### Debug Endpoints ```bash # Check user context curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ https://registry.example.com/api/debug/user-context # List available scopes curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ https://registry.example.com/api/scopes ``` --- ## Server Authentication Credentials The MCP Gateway Registry supports backend server authentication, allowing MCP servers that require their own authentication (API keys, bearer tokens, etc.) to be registered with encrypted credentials. ### Overview When an MCP server requires authentication, you can provide the credentials during registration. The registry: 1. **Encrypts** the credential using Fernet symmetric encryption 2. **Stores** the encrypted credential in MongoDB/DocumentDB 3. **Automatically decrypts** and uses the credential for: - Health checks - Tool discovery and fetching - MCP client connections ### Supported Authentication Schemes | Scheme | Description | Example Use Case | |--------|-------------|------------------| | `none` | No authentication required | Public MCP servers | | `bearer` | Bearer token in `Authorization` header | OAuth2-protected services | | `api_key` | API key with custom header name | Services requiring API keys (e.g., `X-API-Key`, `CONTEXT7_API_KEY`) | ### Credential Encryption All credentials are encrypted before storage using the Fernet encryption scheme: - **Algorithm**: Fernet (symmetric encryption based on AES-128-CBC) - **Key**: Derived from `ENCRYPTION_KEY` environment variable - **Storage**: Encrypted credential stored as `auth_credential_encrypted` in MongoDB - **Decryption**: Automatic during health checks and MCP client initialization **Configuration** (`.env` file): ```bash # Encryption key for server credentials (base64-encoded Fernet key) ENCRYPTION_KEY=your-base64-encoded-fernet-key-here # Generate a new key with: python -c "from cryptography.fernet import Fernet; print(Fernet.generate_key().decode())" ``` ### Registering Servers with Authentication #### Method 1: Registry UI 1. Navigate to **Register Server** in the Registry UI 2. Fill in server details (name, path, proxy URL, etc.) 3. Select **Authentication Scheme**: - `none` - No authentication - `bearer` - Bearer token - `api_key` - API key 4. If `bearer` or `api_key`: - Enter the **credential** (API key or bearer token) - For `api_key`: Specify the **header name** (e.g., `CONTEXT7_API_KEY`, `X-API-Key`) 5. Click **Register** ![Server Registration with Authentication Scheme](img/auth-scheme.gif) The credential is automatically encrypted and stored securely. #### Method 2: REST API **Register server with bearer token:** ```bash curl -X POST https://registry.example.com/api/servers/register \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -F "server_name=My Protected Server" \ -F "path=/my-server" \ -F "proxy_pass_url=http://backend:8000/" \ -F "auth_scheme=bearer" \ -F "auth_credential=my-bearer-token-value" \ -F "description=A server requiring bearer auth" ``` **Register server with API key:** ```bash curl -X POST https://registry.example.com/api/servers/register \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -F "server_name=Context7" \ -F "path=/context7" \ -F "proxy_pass_url=http://context7:8000/" \ -F "auth_scheme=api_key" \ -F "auth_credential=ctx7sk-6dd75bd4-80ef-486e-99ef-b5493df4e578" \ -F "auth_header_name=CONTEXT7_API_KEY" \ -F "description=Context7 LLM context service" ``` **Response:** ```json { "message": "Server registered successfully", "path": "/context7", "server_name": "Context7", "auth_scheme": "api_key", "auth_header_name": "CONTEXT7_API_KEY", "auth_credential_encrypted": true } ``` #### Method 3: CLI Tool (`registry_management.py`) **Register server with credentials:** ```bash # Set up authentication export REGISTRY_URL=https://registry.example.com export REGISTRY_TOKEN=$(cat .token) # Register with API key python3 api/registry_management.py \ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ server-register \ --name "Context7" \ --path "/context7" \ --proxy-pass-url "http://context7:8000/" \ --auth-scheme api_key \ --auth-credential "ctx7sk-6dd75bd4-80ef-486e-99ef-b5493df4e578" \ --auth-header-name "CONTEXT7_API_KEY" \ --description "Context7 LLM context service" # Register with bearer token python3 api/registry_management.py \ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ server-register \ --name "Cloudflare API" \ --path "/cloudflare-api" \ --proxy-pass-url "http://cloudflare-mcp:8000/" \ --auth-scheme bearer \ --auth-credential "my-cloudflare-bearer-token" \ --description "Cloudflare MCP Server" ``` ### Updating Server Credentials Credentials can be updated without re-registering the entire server. #### Method 1: REST API **Update credential endpoint:** ```bash curl -X PUT https://registry.example.com/api/servers/context7/credentials \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "auth_scheme": "api_key", "auth_credential": "new-api-key-value", "auth_header_name": "CONTEXT7_API_KEY" }' ``` **Switch to bearer token:** ```bash curl -X PUT https://registry.example.com/api/servers/my-server/credentials \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "auth_scheme": "bearer", "auth_credential": "new-bearer-token" }' ``` **Remove authentication:** ```bash curl -X PUT https://registry.example.com/api/servers/my-server/credentials \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "auth_scheme": "none" }' ``` **Response:** ```json { "message": "Auth credentials updated successfully", "path": "/context7", "auth_scheme": "api_key", "auth_header_name": "CONTEXT7_API_KEY" } ``` #### Method 2: CLI Tool **Update server credential:** ```bash python3 api/registry_management.py \ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ server-update-credential \ --path "/context7" \ --auth-scheme api_key \ --credential "new-api-key-value" \ --auth-header-name "CONTEXT7_API_KEY" ``` **Update to bearer token:** ```bash python3 api/registry_management.py \ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ server-update-credential \ --path "/cloudflare-api" \ --auth-scheme bearer \ --credential "new-bearer-token-value" ``` **Remove authentication:** ```bash python3 api/registry_management.py \ --registry-url $REGISTRY_URL \ --token $REGISTRY_TOKEN \ server-update-credential \ --path "/my-server" \ --auth-scheme none ``` **Output:** ``` Successfully updated credentials for server '/context7' New auth scheme: api_key Auth header name: CONTEXT7_API_KEY ``` ### How Credentials Are Used #### 1. Health Checks When the health check service performs periodic checks, it: 1. Retrieves the server's `auth_credential_encrypted` from MongoDB 2. Decrypts the credential using the `ENCRYPTION_KEY` 3. Includes the appropriate header in the MCP initialize request: - Bearer: `Authorization: Bearer ` - API Key: `: ` **Example health check with auth:** ```python # Health check service automatically decrypts and uses credentials headers = {} if server.auth_scheme == "bearer": headers["Authorization"] = f"Bearer {decrypt_credential(server.auth_credential_encrypted)}" elif server.auth_scheme == "api_key": header_name = server.auth_header_name or "X-API-Key" headers[header_name] = decrypt_credential(server.auth_credential_encrypted) # MCP initialize request with auth headers response = await mcp_client.initialize(url=server.proxy_pass_url, headers=headers) ``` #### 2. Tool Discovery When fetching tools from a server: 1. Registry decrypts the credential 2. Includes auth headers in the MCP `tools/list` request 3. Stores the fetched tools in the database #### 3. MCP Client Connections When AI coding assistants connect to a server through the gateway: 1. User provides gateway auth token (`X-Authorization` header) 2. Gateway validates user permissions 3. Gateway retrieves and decrypts server credential 4. Gateway proxies the request with the server's auth header **Example MCP client configuration:** ```json { "mcpServers": { "context7": { "type": "streamable-http", "url": "https://mcpgateway.ddns.net/context7/mcp", "disabled": false, "headers": { "X-Authorization": "Bearer ", "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "" } } } } ``` ### Security Considerations 1. **Encryption at Rest**: - All credentials are encrypted in MongoDB using Fernet - Never store plaintext credentials in the database 2. **Key Management**: - Store `ENCRYPTION_KEY` securely (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, etc.) - Never commit encryption keys to version control - Rotate encryption keys periodically 3. **Access Control**: - Only users with `register_service` or `modify_service` permissions can set/update credentials - Credentials are never returned in API responses (only `auth_credential_encrypted` flag) 4. **Audit Logging**: - All credential updates are logged with username and timestamp - Review audit logs regularly for unauthorized changes ### Best Practices 1. **Use Environment-Specific Credentials**: - Development: Use test credentials with limited access - Production: Use production credentials with full access 2. **Rotate Credentials Regularly**: - Use the credential update API/CLI to rotate without downtime - Update credentials before they expire 3. **Monitor Health Checks**: - Watch for "auth-expired" health status - Set up alerts for authentication failures 4. **Document Custom Headers**: - For `api_key` auth, document the required header name - Ensure consistency across environments ### Troubleshooting **Credential Update Fails:** ```bash # Verify server exists curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ https://registry.example.com/api/servers # Check auth scheme is valid # Valid values: none, bearer, api_key ``` **Health Check Shows "auth-expired":** ```bash # Update the credential python3 api/registry_management.py \ server-update-credential \ --path "/my-server" \ --auth-scheme bearer \ --credential "new-valid-token" # Force immediate health check curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ https://registry.example.com/api/servers/my-server/refresh ``` **MCP Client Connection Fails:** - Verify both gateway auth (`X-Authorization`) and server auth headers are present - Check credential hasn't expired - Ensure `auth_header_name` matches server's expectation --- ## Additional Resources - [Authentication Design](design/authentication-design.md) - Detailed auth flow diagrams - [IdP Provider Support](design/idp-provider-support.md) - Provider architecture - [Scopes Management](scopes-mgmt.md) - Scope file format reference - [Auth Management](auth-mgmt.md) - CLI operations guide - [AI Coding Assistants Setup](ai-coding-assistants-setup.md) - Complete setup with backend auth examples

content.text:
I now have a thorough picture from all the key documentation. Here is a comprehensive explanation:

---

## What is the MCP Gateway & Registry?

In one sentence: **it is an enterprise control plane that sits in front of all your AI tools (MCP servers and AI agents), giving every human and AI agent a single authenticated, secured, and searchable entry point to interact with them.**

---

## The Problem It Solves

Modern AI development involves many "MCP servers" — small, specialized tool-servers that give AI agents capabilities like "search Jira", "query a database", "get weather data", "browse the web", etc. Without a central hub, every agent must:

- Know the URL of every tool server
- Manage its own authentication credentials for each one
- Hope those servers are healthy
- Have no way to discover new tools it wasn't initially programmed with

The MCP Gateway & Registry eliminates all of this by acting as a **single front door**.

---

## The Core Architecture

```
Human Users   ───────────────────────────────────────┐
AI Agents     ────────────────────────────────────┐  │
                                                  ▼  ▼
                              ┌────────────────────────────────┐
                              │   NGINX Reverse Proxy          │
                              │   (Authentication Gateway)     │
                              └──────────────┬─────────────────┘
                                             │
                              ┌──────────────▼─────────────────┐
                              │   Registry (FastAPI)           │
                              │   ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌───────┐ │
                              │   │ MCP  │ │Agent │ │Skills │ │
                              │   │Server│ │ Reg  │ │  Reg  │ │
                              │   └──────┘ └──────┘ └───────┘ │
                              │   Auth | Search | Audit | ...  │
                              └──────────────┬─────────────────┘
                                             │
                  ┌──────────────┬───────────┴───────────┐
                  ▼              ▼                        ▼
          MCP Server 1    MCP Server 2 ...       External Systems
          (local/EKS)     (Lambda/API GW)        (DBs, APIs, etc.)
```

The whole system is deployed via Docker Compose (locally) or AWS ECS Fargate with Terraform (production on AWS), behind an ALB and optionally CloudFront.

---

## The Five Core Subsystems

### 1. MCP Server Registry & Reverse Proxy

This is the original heart of the system. MCP servers are registered with a name, a path (e.g., `/cloudflare-docs`), and a backend URL. Once registered:

- NGINX routes all requests through `/cloudflare-docs` to the backend
- The gateway handles auth before the request ever reaches the backend
- Backend server credentials (API keys, bearer tokens) are stored **AES-encrypted** in MongoDB and automatically injected by the gateway — the AI agent never needs to know them
- **Health checks** run on a configurable schedule and report status in real time
- **Version routing**: you can run multiple versions of the same server (v1, v2) behind a single path and switch between them with instant rollback
- **Virtual MCP servers**: you can compose a "virtual" server that aggregates tools from multiple real servers into one logical endpoint
- **5-star ratings**: users can rate servers, with aggregate scores and one vote per user

### 2. AI Agent Registry (A2A Protocol)

Beyond MCP tool servers, the registry also manages **AI agents** using the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. An AI agent (e.g., a "Code Reviewer Agent" or "Flight Booking Agent") registers itself with:
- Its capabilities and skills
- Its endpoint URL
- Tags, metadata, ownership info

Once registered, other agents and human users can **discover** it, query what it can do, and call it — all through the registry's API, with the same authentication and access control as MCP servers.

This enables **decentralized agent networks**: instead of a central orchestrator knowing about all agents, any agent can query the registry to find the right agent for a task at runtime.

**Team isolation** is built in: a `lob1-bot` (Line of Business 1) can only see agents in its own group; it cannot see LOB2 agents even if they exist.

### 3. Agent Skills Registry

A third registry layer specifically for **SKILL.md files** — a Claude Code feature where markdown files define behavioral instructions for AI coding assistants. These skills can be:
- Registered with a URL to their `SKILL.md` file
- Searched semantically
- Security-scanned before being made available
- Versioned and toggled on/off

This lets enterprise teams share and control approved behavioral recipes for AI coding assistants.

### 4. Authentication & Fine-Grained Authorization

Every request (human or agent) flows through NGINX's `auth_request` directive to the **Auth Server** before reaching the registry. The system supports:

**Three identity types:**
| Who | How they authenticate | Token lifetime |
|-----|----------------------|----------------|
| Human users (browser) | OAuth2 Authorization Code via Keycloak or Microsoft Entra ID | 8-hour session cookie |
| Developers / CLI / AI coding assistants | Self-signed JWT (HS256) generated via the UI | 8 hours |
| AI agents (M2M) | OAuth2 Client Credentials (service account) | 1 hour, must refresh |

**Fine-grained scopes** control exactly what each group can do:
```json
// "lob1-users" can only call specific tools on one specific server:
{
  "server_access": [
    {"server": "context7", "methods": ["tools/call"], "tools": ["search"]}
  ]
}

// "registry-admins" can do everything:
{
  "server_access": [{"server": "*", "methods": ["all"], "tools": ["all"]}]
}
```

This is enforced at the NGINX layer — the request is killed before it hits the FastAPI app if permissions are insufficient.

### 5. Security Scanning

Every MCP server, AI agent, and skill is automatically **security-scanned at registration time** using three open-source tools from Cisco AI Defense:

| Target | Scanner | What it detects |
|--------|---------|-----------------|
| MCP servers | `cisco-ai-defense/mcp-scanner` | SQL injection, XSS, command injection, prompt injection, path traversal, hardcoded credentials in tool definitions |
| A2A agents | `cisco-ai-defense/a2a-scanner` | YARA pattern matching + heuristic threat detection on agent cards |
| Skills (SKILL.md) | `cisco-ai-defense/cisco-ai-skill-scanner` | Prompt injection, privilege escalation, social engineering, malicious URL references |

**If a scan fails:**
1. The item is registered but **disabled** (agents and users can't reach it)
2. It gets a `security-pending` tag
3. A detailed JSON report is saved
4. An admin must review and manually enable it

**Periodic registry-wide scans** can also be run to catch newly discovered threats against already-registered servers.

Two analyzer modes are available: **YARA** (fast, pattern-based, free) and **LLM** (slower, semantic, needs an API key), which can be combined for maximum coverage.

---

## Two Killer Features for AI Agents

### Dynamic Tool Discovery

Agents don't need to know what tools exist in advance. They can call the **`intelligent_tool_finder`** tool with a natural language query:

```
"I need to know the current time in Tokyo"
→ registry searches vector embeddings of all tool descriptions
→ returns: {"tool_name": "current_time_by_timezone", "server": "/currenttime", "score": 0.89}
→ agent calls invoke_mcp_tool with that info
→ gets the answer
```

Under the hood this uses FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) with sentence-transformer embeddings over all registered tool descriptions, performing cosine similarity ranking. The index rebuilds automatically when servers are added or changed.

This makes AI agents **adaptively extensible** — they can handle tasks they were never explicitly programmed for, as long as the right tool exists somewhere in the registry.

### Federated Registry Import

The registry can pull in tools/agents from external sources automatically:

| Source | What it imports | Auth |
|--------|----------------|------|
| Anthropic's official MCP registry | Curated public MCP servers | None required |
| Workday ASOR | Enterprise AI agents | OAuth2 |
| Peer registries (other instances of this system) | Bidirectional server + agent sync | Static token or OAuth2 |

Peer-to-peer federation lets multiple organizations or teams each run their own registry instance but share a subset of their servers/agents with each other via configurable sync modes (all, whitelist, or tag-filtered).

---

## What It Runs On

| Component | Technology |
|-----------|-----------|
| API/backend | Python 3.11+, FastAPI, async |
| Database | MongoDB Community Edition (local) or AWS DocumentDB (production) |
| Reverse proxy | NGINX (dynamically configured by the registry) |
| Auth | Keycloak or Microsoft Entra ID (pluggable) |
| Search | HNSW vector index in MongoDB/DocumentDB, FAISS in-memory |
| Embeddings | Sentence-transformers (local) or Amazon Bedrock Titan / OpenAI via LiteLLM |
| Frontend | React web app |
| Observability | Prometheus + Grafana dashboards + OpenTelemetry |
| Deployment | Docker Compose (local), Terraform + AWS ECS Fargate (production) |
| CI | GitHub Actions, 850+ pytest tests |

---

## Who Uses It and How

| User | They do this |
|------|-------------|
| **Admin (human)** | Registers MCP servers, creates OAuth scopes for teams, reviews security scan results, monitors health |
| **Developer** | Gets a JWT token from the UI, configures VS Code / Cursor / Claude Code to use the gateway as their MCP endpoint |
| **AI agent** | Authenticates with a service account (M2M), queries the tool registry, invokes tools through the gateway, or discovers and calls other agents |
| **CI/CD pipeline** | Uses a static API key to programmatically register/deregister servers during deployments |

---

## The Big Picture Summary

The MCP Gateway & Registry is the **"service mesh for AI tools"** — the same way Kubernetes + Istio manages microservices communication at scale (auth, discovery, routing, health, observability), this system does the same for the ecosystem of MCP tool servers and AI agents. It is particularly valuable when you have:

- Many teams, each with different tools, that should not all see each other's resources
- AI agents that need to operate autonomously without hardcoded tool knowledge
- Security and compliance requirements around what tools AI systems can access
- A need to centrally audit all AI tool invocations

The user is asking two questions:

1. Why would we prefer to add a `len(expression)` check to the safe calculator?
2. Why didn't we use `ast.literal_eval()` as suggested in the issue itself?

Let me address both clearly.

**Why `ast.literal_eval()` doesn't work:**
`ast.literal_eval()` only evaluates Python literals - strings, numbers, tuples, lists, dicts, sets, booleans, and None. It does NOT evaluate expressions. So `ast.literal_eval("2 + 2")` raises a `ValueError` because `2 + 2` is a `BinOp` (binary operation node), not a literal. The issue's suggested remediation is simply wrong/incomplete for this use case.
 
**Why add `len(expression)` check:**
The security reviewer flagged a potential DoS vulnerability where expressions like `9**9**9**9**9` pass the regex validation but would consume massive amounts of CPU and memory during evaluation. A length limit on the input string is a straightforward, low-cost defense against this kind of attack.

content.text:
Two separate questions — good ones.

---

### Why not `ast.literal_eval()`?

The issue's suggested remediation is **wrong for this use case**. `ast.literal_eval()` only evaluates Python *literals* — raw values like `42`, `"hello"`, `[1, 2, 3]`. It does **not** evaluate expressions.

Try it yourself:

```python
>>> import ast
>>> ast.literal_eval("2 + 2")
ValueError: malformed node or string on line 1: <ast.BinOp object at 0x...>
```

`2 + 2` is parsed as an `ast.BinOp` node (a binary operation), not a literal. `ast.literal_eval` sees that and immediately raises. The `calculator()` function needs to evaluate arithmetic *expressions* — by definition, `ast.literal_eval()` cannot do that.

The issue author copied the standard Bandit remediation advice for `eval()` without checking whether it actually fits this specific call site. It works fine when `eval()` is being used to parse a config value or deserialize data, but not here.

---

### Why add `len(expression) > 200`?

Even with the safe AST evaluator in place (no arbitrary code execution), there's still a **denial-of-service** vector that the AST approach alone doesn't close.

The regex allowlist `r'^[0-9+\-*/().^ ]+$'` permits `*` characters. After the `^` → `**` substitution, a user can submit:

```
9**9**9**9**9**9
```

This passes the regex, passes the AST node whitelist (`ast.Constant` and `ast.Pow` only), and Python will begin computing it. The result has millions of digits and takes essentially forever (or exhausts memory). The AST evaluator gives you *safety* (no code injection) but not *boundedness* (no runaway computation).

`len(expression) > 200` is the simplest possible mitigation: it costs one comparison and gives you a hard upper bound on input size before any parsing or computation happens. It doesn't need to be clever — 200 characters is already far more than any legitimate `"5 * (3 + 4) / 2.0"` expression needs. Anything longer is almost certainly either malformed or adversarial.

A more precise fix would cap the operand values inside `_safe_eval_arithmetic()` itself (e.g., reject any `ast.Constant` node with value `> 10**15`), but the length check is cheap, obvious, and catches the attack before it ever reaches the evaluator.

content.thinking:
The bandit scan found more issues than described in the issue. Let me analyze what we found vs what's in the issue:

**B307 (eval)**: 1 occurrence at `agents/agent.py:406` — matches the issue (line number slightly off, now 406 not 401, probably due to added code)

**B310 (urllib_urlopen)**: 
- `cli/mcp_utils.py:192` — matches issue
- `tests/e2e/test_virtual_mcp_latency.py:148` — NEW, not in original issue
- `tests/e2e/test_virtual_mcp_protocol.py:118` — NEW


- `tests/e2e/test_virtual_mcp_protocol.py:161` — NEW
- `tests/e2e/test_virtual_mcp_stress.py:112` — NEW

**B404 (import_subprocess)**: The issue says 16, but we found more across multiple files including `api/registry_client.py:16`, `api/registry_management.py:226`, `cli/agent_mgmt.py:54`, `cli/mcp_security_scanner.py:14`, and `cli/scan_all_servers.py:20`.

Looking through the rest of the codebase, there are additional subprocess imports scattered throughout the credentials provider, registry services, and test files that also need to be addressed. I see there's a `.bandit` configuration file that might be excluding certain paths, but the current scan is picking up test files that weren't in the original issue. The extra findings are all in the `tests/e2e/` and `tests/unit/` directories, which use urllib and subprocess for testing purposes. Let me clarify what's actually new versus what was already reported.

content.text: Yes, all the issues are confirmed — and there are actually **more than the issue documented**. Let me break it down:

input.description: Check what .bandit config is doing

content.content:
[bandit]
# Exclude test directories and virtual environment from Bandit scans
# B101 (assert_used) only appears in test code; excluding test dirs resolves it
# For pyproject.toml config (used by pre-commit), see [tool.bandit] in pyproject.toml
exclude = ./tests,./agents/a2a/test,./metrics-service/tests,./.venv

toolUseResult.stdout:
[bandit]

content.thinking:
Interesting! The `.bandit` config excludes `./tests`, but since we ran with `--exclude ./.venv,./frontend/node_modules`, we overrode that config. Let me re-run with just the config file respected to see what the "official" scope is.

Wait, actually when you specify tests on the command line with `bandit -r .`, it uses both the `.bandit` file exclusions AND any command line exclusions. But I ran `bandit -r . -t B404,B307,B310 --exclude ./.venv,./frontend/node_modules` which overrides the config file's exclude setting.


I should run it again respecting the `.bandit` config to see what the CI-relevant scope actually includes.

content.text: The `.bandit` config excludes `./tests` — our scan overrode that. Let me re-run respecting the config so we see what CI actually sees:

input.command: uv run bandit -r . -t B404,B307,B310 --exclude ./.venv,./frontend/node_modules,./tests 2>&1 | grep -E "^>>|Location:|^Code|^Run|Total issues|Low:|Medium:|High:"
